“Number Three” is a sad but predictable story of #cryptocurrency centralised trading and lending businesses blowing up due to too much leverage. Long Live #DeFi. SOFYL https://cryptohayes.medium.com/ ... https://twitter.com/...
Read!! How 2AC blew a whale shark-sized hole in crypto markets with reputation based leverage across centralized lending platforms https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ...

“When you remove trust from the equation and rely purely on transparent lending standards executed by impartial computer code, you get a better outcome. This is the lesson to be learned” -@CryptoHayes's essay on 3AC & ecosystem is my best read this week! https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ...

“Both centralised and decentralised lending companies / platforms had exposure to 3AC — and only the players in one of these two markets went belly up. The centralised lenders failed en masse, while their decentralised counterparts...” ~ @CryptoHayes https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ...

Clear (and humorous) recap from @CryptoHayes on: - 3AC and its collapse - The subsequent fallout on CeFi companies - “The [DeFi] protocols... did not have to halt any withdrawals... continued to issue loans... did not suffer any downtime.” https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ...

Latest from @CryptoHayes is excellent: “Very few money managers stopped to engage the critical thinking part of their brain and ponder how the fuggity fuck Anchor generated these yields.” https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ...

Another excellent blog by @CryptoHayes - despite the market carnage, it has been encouraging to see how well DeFi protocols fared. I hope that regulators and the public see how DeFi can lead to better outcomes for lenders, borrowers, and investors. https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ... https://twitter.com/...

“Both centralised & decentralised lending companies/ platforms had exposure to 3AC - & only the players in one of these two markets went belly up.” Great analysis of the Three Arrows Capital (3AC) blowup and how decentralized platforms faired much better. https://cryptohayes.medium.com/ ...

The crypto carnage was driven by centralized lenders with weak risk controls and minimal collateral requirements Defi lending protocols operated as designed, and just notched another proof point through a catastrophic risk contagion 👇 https://twitter.com/...

This was a good read on how crypto broke, but I'm still left wondering why people are so excited about lending that requires 150%+ collateral. Is the TAM huge for this? Does crypto expand the market or materially lower costs? DeFi survived, so what? https://entrepreneurshandbook.co/ ... https://twitter.com/...

An excellent article by BitMEX founder Arthur Hayes reviewing the character and behavior patterns of the founders of 3AC: The only way to juice the performance of your hedge fund is to become a directional trader, and/or use borrowed funds. 3AC did both . https://cryptohayes.medium.com/ ...

Google to Delete Abortion-Clinic Visits From Location History
Google will start auto-deleting health clinic location data
Fun fact: Apple already does this. Calls it “location fuzzing” https://twitter.com/... https://twitter.com/...
Location data shouldn't be collected in the first place. The right to privacy is meaningless if it can be adjudicated by a private company. https://twitter.com/...

I think instead of Google selectively deleting whether you visited an abortion climic or a rehab from location history, it should delete or encrypt everything. Seems weird for privacy to depend on if a PM decided a particular business is sensitive or not. https://www.bloomberg.com/...
👋🏼 @Apple, @TMobile and other carriers: your move now. https://twitter.com/...
Your reaction to this should not be praise for Google, it should be concern that a private company has this power. https://twitter.com/...

I know I'm not the first to point this out but this change is the epitome of “too little too late”. The extent of this data collection and selling should never have occurred in the first place. Scant details of how and when (read: whether) it will end. https://blog.google/...
The absence of data is data. “Why does your location data stop for an hour?” Also Google are an advertising company https://support.google.com/... https://twitter.com/...

Google announces it will not retain location data regarding users' visits to “counseling centers, domestic violence shelters, abortion clinics, fertility centers, addiction treatment facilities, weight loss clinics, cosmetic surgery clinics, and others.” https://blog.google/...

Google says it will delete your location data if you visit an abortion clinic, amid surveillance concerns post-Roe & reminds us — you can always turn off your location tracking all together ! https://www.politico.com/...

People don't think about this: unless you take action, Google (and Apple and carriers) have complete and precise historical location data about you. It can be helpful! But sometimes not. Auto-deleting sensitive location is a good move by @google to improve privacy & reduce risk. https://twitter.com/...

I'm also curious about how this works in practice. Does it mean that Google is now creating a massive database of locations they consider to be “sensitive” and then using some kind of automation to set different retention practices for that data than other data? That seems... bad

Google also making it easier to delete your menstruation tracking information from Fitbit. This statement DOES NOT specifically say how they will respond to abortion-related data requests from users, something they are under pressure to clarify their stance on

It is scary to me the extragovernmental authority that big tech has over our lives - with no accountability to make sure that this is happening. Still, a good move for Google. Can we take its word? https://twitter.com/...

In June, @freepress & 50 civil rights allies urged @Google to stop collecting location data. Google just announced it will delete location history for users visiting places sensitive to their health. Big win! Next: stop collecting all our data, @Google! https://blog.google/...

This is good in the sense it shows tech companies are feeling the heat to clean up the surveillance hellscape mess they've made. But Google is going to have to do a whole lot more than this if they want to prevent their data hoard from being weaponized against abortion access https://twitter.com/...

Google says it will auto-delete location history for people who visit abortion clinics, as well as other sensitive locations such as fertility centers, addiction treatment facilities, and domestic violence shelters. https://blog.google/...

(Long thread!) Excited to share an update on our previously announced term sheet with @FTX_US - and how we've broadened the scope of the initial deal for the benefit of all key @BlockFi stakeholders.

Yesterday we signed definitive agreements, subject to shareholder approval, with FTX US for: 1. A $400M revolving credit facility which is subordinate to all client funds, and 2. An option to acquire BlockFi at a variable price of up to $240M based on performance triggers.

Basically, Sam Bankman-Fried bails out all the systemically important crypto banks and forms an entity to oversee them, which will provide emergency loans at discounted rates in times of extraordinary stress. Kinda like a central bank.

FTX US is looking forward to working further with @BlockFiZac and the @BlockFi team! We're excited help bolster BlockFi's business and work together on paths towards strategic partnership! https://twitter.com/...
Really excited to work with @BlockFi https://twitter.com/...
I am genuinely confused how you sell for pennies on the dollar and the post-mortem characterizes it as a minor oopsie. https://twitter.com/...

Still think the BlockFi 🤝 FTX US deal is a big win for customers (for now) Still think the overall risk contagion is far from over Dominoes are still falling. Sharks still swimming 🦈 https://twitter.com/...

Two lawyerly questions: 1. Why ‘subject to shareholder approval’ if you're two priv companies? It takes 5 mins to sort... 2. What are the ‘performance triggers’? Blockfi is loss making... Seems like @BlockFi shareholders get paid UP TO 5% of last valuation round. Brutal. https://twitter.com/...
Binance's CEO @cz_binance says that Binance is having acquisition conversations with more than 50 crypto companies https://blockworks.co/...

Tl;dr > Net 3AC loss to BlockFi ~$80mm > Equity valuation likely down 90%+ from $3b > Credit line ($400m) and higher interest to increase mkt confidence > no halt in withdrawals and retail wins > more FTX + BlockFi collab Well played @SBF_FTX @BlockFiZac @BlockFi 👍👏 https://twitter.com/...

BlockFi Strikes Credit Deal With FTX US, Outlines Acquisition Path - $400 million revolving credit line - option to acquire the firm for up to $240 million - Three Arrows Capital's liquidation resulted in $80 million loss for BlockFi https://www.theblock.co/...

Actual price for BlockFi is “up to $240M” depending on performance + a $400m revolving credit facility. Still a far cry from the $3B a year ago (& we have no idea what's in the performance triggers and whether they will hit them)... https://twitter.com/...

Let's unpick this. It consists of two elements: - a call warrant with a variable strike price conditional on unspecified performance triggers - a subordinated credit facility. Both are bad news for ordinary shareholders. https://twitter.com/...

The deal represents a discount to BlockFi's $3 billion valuation as of March 2021. BlockFi CEO Zac Prince said the company had about $80 million in losses from bad debt of Three Arrows https://www.bloomberg.com/... https://twitter.com/...
“up to” doing a lot of work here https://twitter.com/...
You thought the crypto boom was over, but BlockFi's valuation went up almost 10x in a day! https://www.axios.com/...
This is good news for the industry IMO. It removes uncertainty. https://twitter.com/...
So the earlier leak was way off, $240M not $25M, and with a $400M credit facility. https://twitter.com/...

It's easy to criticize others but this seems to be a very good outcome for Clients. The stakeholders ( Investors/Founders/Employees etc) will absorb the losses. ( Disclosure: I've no relationship with BFi) https://twitter.com/...
This is actually a pretty favorable outcome all things considered. https://twitter.com/...
How does an $80m loss on a company that's raised $1 billion lead to a firesale? This is certainly not the whole truth. https://twitter.com/...
it's just an option but still, blockfi has raised more than $2.1 *billion* from investors, so they're basically wiped out https://twitter.com/...

Just to cut through the noise: + BlockFi took $80mm loss on 3AC + The issue was *fear* of a bank run + BlockFi's unwillingness to suspend withdrawals which led to a sale ("customers before company" broke the bank) + FTX deal locks in capital; upside for BlockFi principals

JUST IN: BlockFi and FTX US reach deal that gives the exchange the right to acquire the crypto lender at a valuation up to $240 million, based on “performance triggers.” @realDannyNelson reports https://www.coindesk.com/...
“up to” so still could sell for $25M, lol https://twitter.com/...
Again... We have zero clue how much of the $240 million is guaranteed. https://twitter.com/...

It's official: FTX has a deal with BlockFi. $240 million is high end of price range (as we reported earlier, all the deals include earnouts). No word on the low end, which may well be that $25m. No matter what, a huge loss for the investors.

HackerOne says an employee stole vulnerability reports submitted through its bug bounty platform and disclosed them to seven companies for financial rewards
A HackerOne Employee Stole Vulnerability Reports From Security Researchers
Daily Drop (182)  —  Sunday, July 03, 2022 // (IG): BB //Weekly Sponsor: Zanes Hand Made (leather works)
How Bug Bounty Platform HackerOne Handled Its Own ‘Internal Threat’ Actor

HackerOne discloses security incident. The company says that a “then-employee had improperly accessed security reports for personal gain” and shared details about a vulnerability outside the platform https://hackerone.com/... https://twitter.com/...
Imagine submitting a bug report, watching it sit pending review to be closed as a dupe for someone to take your entire bug report and collect your bounty. https://twitter.com/...

Still waiting for one that will sell reports as zero-days to threat actors. With bug bounty platforms getting increasingly popular, it's only a matter of time. And it will be way harder to detect and to trace than it was here. https://twitter.com/...

If DoorDash allowed drivers to query customer details of everyone signed up, not just their active order, people would lose their minds. But bug bounty employees accessing critical vulns of customers they aren't even working on? Just a policy stopping it, no technical controls https://twitter.com/...

Crazy. I always made jokes about this and finally has happened. Kudos to hackerone for their work on identifying this quickly. Makes me wonder about the scenarios that don't get caught. This is for sure a type of threat that all companies with BB programs need to watch https://twitter.com/...
Kinda interesting - somebody mentioned security researchers selling their bug bounty exploits on dark web in parallel, but maybe it was this. https://hackerone.com/...

Insider threats affect every business. Transparency is praiseworthy. Yet conveniently forgetting that “policy & contractual” controls are not the same as technical access controls every time something comes up around triage access abuse is its own open bug requiring resolution. https://twitter.com/...

Another example of the danger of insider threats. Company identified the culprit only when a customer received from the rogue employee a bug report similar to one that had been submitted through HackerOne on the same vuln. https://twitter.com/...
Malicious insiders, a nightmare for a company. https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/ ... #infosec

We've been criticized for not having a 3rd party run our bug bounty program but this is exactly why we run it ourselves. Resubmitting bugs for the bounty is the least bad thing that can happen. Worst case, the stolen bugs are sold to more serious hackers or directly exploited. https://twitter.com/...

This might be the first public headline of something like this that I've seen but there are many a large companies without proper authn/authz on their bug databases and not the first stolen / resold bugs. https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/ ...

Yea, we have been saying this for years. Marking a report critical then dropping it to informational, and then marking it as a duplicate and not letting you see the duplicate...I've been saying @FBI needs to investigate @Hacker0x01 for a long time. https://twitter.com/...
Frontrunning their own bug bounty is not something I expected to see... https://twitter.com/...
